nativeRange.habitat: Found across pan-tropical coral reefs and rocky reef habitats, where it typically hides upside-down in caves, under ledges, and inside crevices. It extends its long white antennae out into open water to advertise itself as a cleaning station, where passing fish come to have parasites and debris removed.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with caves, overhangs and crevices for hiding and cleaning stations
a defined territory of rockwork that the shrimp can claim and return to
open ledges where fish can approach to be cleaned
stable, well-aged reef system (intolerant of unstable parameters)

diet.notes: Primarily a scavenger and opportunistic carnivore in the wild, but readily accepts most aquarium foods in captivity, including frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, sinking pellets and flake. Often documented eating bristle worms, making it a useful pest-control invertebrate. Spends much of the day hiding, so targeted feeding (e.g., a small piece of food dropped near its cave) is commonly advised to ensure it gets its share. Periodic iodine and trace-element supplementation is generally recommended to support molting.

goodWith: most peaceful and medium-sized reef fish
tangs, wrasses and other fish that visit cleaning stations
clownfish and damselfish of similar size
LPS, SPS, soft corals and zoanthids (with adequate feeding)
reef-safe snails and similarly sized hermit crabs
avoidWith: other Coral Banded Shrimp (except as a true bonded pair purchased together small)
smaller ornamental shrimp (e.g. Lysmata cleaner shrimp, sexy shrimp, peppermint shrimp)
very small fish that could be ambushed at night
hawkfish, lionfish, large wrasses, triggerfish and pufferfish (will eat the shrimp)
large predatory crabs and mantis shrimp
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: The Coral Banded Shrimp (Stenopus hispidus) is a pan-tropical cleaner shrimp recognized by its bold red-and-white bands, extra-long white antennae, and oversized claws. Generally considered hardy, it is highly aggressive toward other shrimp and is best kept solo or as a true bonded pair.
careNotes: The Coral Banded Shrimp is among the most widely recognised marine ornamental shrimp, distinguished by its bold red-and-white bands, long whip-like white antennae, and conspicuous oversized third-leg claws that give rise to the 'boxer' nickname. It is generally considered hardy and a reasonable choice for a first saltwater invertebrate, though its behavioral tendencies add practical complexity. The species is highly aggressive toward others of its own kind: two individuals kept together will typically fight unless they form a true bonded pair, which is most reliably achieved by introducing two small specimens at the same time and allowing them to pair naturally. Same-sex pairings almost always end in the death of one animal. Established pairs that are separated or relocated often turn on each other when reintroduced. Despite the 'banded cleaner shrimp' trade name, cleaning behavior in captivity is variable — some individuals regularly service tankmates, while others largely ignore them. The species is broadly considered reef-safe but with important caveats: it has been documented as a useful predator of bristle worms, but may also opportunistically target very small ornamental shrimp, small slow-moving fish at night, and occasionally pinch at corals or steal food from coral mouths. Like all marine invertebrates, it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications; any history of copper treatment in the system should be considered disqualifying. Drip acclimation over an extended period is commonly advised to avoid salinity and pH shock. Note: this species is not to be confused with Lybia tessellata (the pom-pom crab), which is sometimes marketed under the name 'boxer crab'.
breedingNotes: Forms long-term monogamous pairs in the wild, with females typically larger than males and pairs defending a 1–2 m diameter territory. Females brood greenish eggs attached to the pleopods and pairs alternate molting cycles in synchrony. Eggs hatch into planktonic larvae that pass through many zoeal stages, and captive rearing is widely regarded as very difficult — larvae are almost always lost to filtration, skimming, or starvation in standard reef setups. Successful rearing has been documented but generally requires a dedicated kreisel or planktonic-larvae system with appropriate live foods.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied LiveAquaria root resolved to this product page via search. Provided baseline husbandry parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), reef-safe yes, copper intolerance and iodine-supported molting.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 and could not be accessed; swapped to other reef-aquarium care guides per playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (5–7 cm), water parameters (22–27°C, SG 1.020–1.025), 20-gallon minimum tank size, omnivorous diet, monthly molting cadence and difficult breeding.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry guide: 22–27°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, kH 8–12, 20 gal minimum, ~22–26 day molt cycle, severe conspecific aggression with mated pairs as the workaround, and reef-safe-with-caution behaviour around soft corals.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan around 2–3 years typical (with documented cases exceeding 9 years), peaceful behaviour with most fish, and incompatibility with shrimp-eating predators (lionfish, hawkfish, triggers).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Stenopus hispidus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Stenopodidae, order Decapoda), pan-tropical distribution (Western Atlantic, Indo-Pacific, Red Sea, Australia, New Zealand), depth range to 210 m, monogamous pair-bonding and cleaner-shrimp biology.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults generally molt roughly every three to four weeks (reported around 22–26 days); juveniles molt more often. The shrimp typically hides for 1–2 days before and after a molt and is vulnerable during this window. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium and especially iodine and trace-element levels support successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most commonly cited cause of failed molts. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Forms monogamous pairs; females brood greenish eggs on the pleopods and pairs alternate molts in synchrony. Planktonic larvae are extremely difficult to rear in standard reef setups due to long larval duration, sensitivity, and loss to filtration and skimming. Successful captive rearing generally requires a dedicated planktonic-larvae system.
